elements.sent-2411-001: We consider the problem of selecting a committee of $k$ alternatives among $m$ alternatives, based on the ordinal rank list of voters.
elements.sent-2411-002: Our focus is on the case where both voters and alternatives lie on a metric space—specifically, on the line—and the objective is to minimize the additive social cost.
elements.sent-2411-003: The additive social cost is the sum of the costs for all voters, where the cost for each voter is defined as the sum of their distances to each member of the selected committee.
elements.sent-2411-004: We propose a new voting rule, the Polar Comparison Rule, which achieves upper bounds of $1 + \sqrt{2 \approx 2.41$ and $7/3 \approx 2.33$ distortions for $k = 2$ and $k = 3$, respectively, and we show that these bounds are tight.}
elements.sent-2411-005: Furthermore, we generalize this rule, showing that it maintains a distortion of roughly $7/3$ based on the remainder of the committee size when divided by three.
elements.sent-2411-006: We also establish lower bounds on the achievable distortion based on the parity of $k$ and for both small and large committee sizes.
elements.sent-2411-007: Imagine a city council election where residents must select a committee of $3$ representatives from $9$ candidates.
elements.sent-2411-008: Voters have cardinal utilities or costs for each candidate, but only submit ordinal rankings based on these values.
elements.sent-2411-009: Using these rankings, the representatives are chosen by a voting rule.
elements.sent-2411-010: The goal is to design a voting rule that ensures the selected committee fairly represents the electorate's diverse preferences.
elements.sent-2411-011: If the voting rule had access to the cardinal values, selecting the optimal outcome would be less challenging.
elements.sent-2411-012: For example, in a single-winner election, the voting rule could simply choose the candidate that maximizes social welfare or minimizes social cost.
elements.sent-2411-013: However, assuming access to cardinal values is often impractical.
elements.sent-2411-014: Voters may struggle to accurately quantify their preferences, and asking for such detailed information may lead to inconsistencies or reluctance.
elements.sent-2411-015: Additionally, the cost and complexity of collecting precise cardinal values make it infeasible in large-scale elections.
elements.sent-2411-016: As a result, ordinal rankings are typically used instead.
elements.sent-2411-017: The efficiency gap due to the lack of cardinal information is captured by distortion \citep{procaccia2006distortion .}
elements.sent-2411-018: In single-winner elections, distortion of a voting rule $f$ is defined as the worst-case ratio (across all instances) between the social welfare (or social cost) of the candidate selected by $f$ and that of the optimal candidate, which is based on hidden cardinal values.\footnote{We refer to Section \ref{sec:prelim for a formal definition of distortion.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Distortion is usually studied in two main frameworks: the utilitarian framework and the metric framework.
elements.sent-2411-020: In the utilitarian framework, cardinal values are utilities that often sum to one for each voter, and in the metric framework, cardinal values are costs that satisfy metric properties such as the triangle inequality.
elements.sent-2411-021: There are many arguments that justify why these two scenarios are reasonable and applicable to real-world situations \citep{aziz2020justifications,jessee2012ideology,enelow1984spatial,merrill1999unified .}
elements.sent-2411-022: For example, the line metric can model political preferences along a political spectrum, ranging from liberal to conservative.
elements.sent-2411-023: The distortion in both utilitarian and metric frameworks is very well understood for single-winner elections.
elements.sent-2411-024: In the utilitarian case, any voting rule has a distortion of at least \(\Omega(m^2)\), with the plurality rule reaching this bound \citep{caragiannis2011voting .}
elements.sent-2411-025: In the metric framework, the lower bound for any deterministic rule is $3$ \citep{ANSHELEVICH201827 , and rules like Plurality Veto \citep{kizilkaya2022plurality and Plurality Matching \citep{gkatzelis2020resolving achieve this distortion.}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Additionally, tight bounds have been established for many voting rules in both frameworks \citep{ANSHELEVICH201827 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: However, moving from single-winner to multi-winner elections introduces more challenges.
elements.sent-2411-028: Indeed, even assessing the efficiency of the selected committee can be tricky.
elements.sent-2411-029: For example, consider the instance in Figure \ref{fig:ub(intro) , and assume the goal is to select $3$ candidates.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Choosing $b_1, b_2, b_3$ minimizes total social cost, but may not fairly represent voter preferences.
elements.sent-2411-031: Alternatively, selecting $a_1, b_1, c_1$ increases the total social cost but ensures each voter’s top choice is included.
elements.sent-2411-032: Therefore, different criteria may lead to different optimal outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-033: Some well-known criteria for multi-winner voting rules includes total social cost of the winners \citep{10.1145/3230654.3230658 , cost of the $q$'th nearest candidate in the committee \citep{caragiannis2022metric, Chen_Li_Wang_2020, cheng2020group , total cost of each voter for the first $q$-nearest candidates in the committee, and more generally, social cost based on arbitrary scoring over the committee as suggested by \cite{caragiannis2022metric .}}}

elements.sent-2411-035: It has been shown by \cite{10.1145/3230654.3230658 that repeatedly applying any single-winner rule with distortion $\delta$ results in a distortion of at most $\delta$.}

elements.sent-2411-040: In this paper, we explore the problem of selecting a committee of $k$ alternatives from a set of ordinal rank lists provided by voters, aiming to minimize the additive social cost for all voters.
elements.sent-2411-041: Our focus is on the case where both voters and alternatives are positioned on a line metric.
elements.sent-2411-042: For an overview of our results and techniques, see Section \ref{resatec .}
elements.sent-2411-043: We introduce a new voting rule and provide improved upper and lower bounds for committee selection with general $k$ alternatives.
elements.sent-2411-044: We show that the alternative with the minimum distance to all voters is either the nearest alternative to the right or the nearest alternative to the left of the median voter.
elements.sent-2411-045: To leverage this insight, we design the Polar Comparison Rule, which prioritizes committees that include alternatives from both sides of the median voter, thereby increasing the likelihood of selecting better alternatives.
elements.sent-2411-046: The Polar Comparison Rule achieves upper bounds of $1 + \sqrt{2 \approx 2.41$ and $7/3 \approx 2.33$ distortions for $k = 2$ and $k = 3$, respectively, and we show that these bounds are tight.}
elements.sent-2411-047: We further extend these results by showing how different voting rules can be effectively combined to improve the bounds across various committee sizes.
elements.sent-2411-048: We generalize our rule and show that it maintains a distortion of $7/3$ for committee sizes divisible by three.
elements.sent-2411-049: For committee sizes where $k \equiv 1 \pmod{3}$, the distortion is $7/3 + 4(\sqrt{2 - 4/3)/k$, and for committee sizes where $k \equiv 2 \pmod{3}$, the distortion is $7/3 + 2(\sqrt{2 - 4/3)/k$.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: A key technical ingredient in our proofs is the fact that we can determine the exact order of the alternatives on the line if we remove all alternatives that are Pareto-dominated by another.
elements.sent-2411-051: Determining the exact order of the alternatives on the line metric is independent of any specific objective function, and we believe this method can be useful in the broader literature.
elements.sent-2411-052: Finally, we complement our results with lower bounds.
elements.sent-2411-053: For each lower bound, we provide two instances where voters share the same priority list and demonstrate a lower bound that any voting rule must satisfy.
elements.sent-2411-054: We establish a lower bound of $2 + 1/k$ for odd values of $k$ and a lower bound of $1 + \sqrt{1 + 2/k}$ for even values of $k$, when $k < m/2$.
elements.sent-2411-055: Moreover, for larger committee sizes where $k \ge m/2$, we provide a lower bound of $1 + (m - k)/(3k - m)$.
elements.sent-2411-056: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: Section \ref{sec:prelim provides the preliminaries.}
elements.sent-2411-057: In Section \ref{sec:order , we show how to determine the exact order of alternatives on the line.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Section \ref{sec:optimal-alternative presents properties of the optimal alternative.}
elements.sent-2411-059: In Section \ref{sec:2-winner , as a warm-up, we explain the high-level idea of our voting rule for $2$-winner elections.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Section \ref{sec:multi-winner extends these results for general values of $k$ by explaining how to combine different voting rules and extends our Polar Comparison Rule for $k=3$, while also providing lower bounds for general values of $k$.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Finally, Section \ref{sec:conclusion discusses extensions of our rule and open questions for future research.}
elements.sent-2411-062: As mentioned, distortion was first introduced by \cite{procaccia2006distortion in the context of the utilitarian framework.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Since then, several lower and upper bounds have been established for distortion across different voting rules, in various scenarios, and within both utilitarian and metric frameworks.
elements.sent-2411-064: For a comprehensive overview of distortion results, we refer to \citep{anshelevich2021distortion .}
elements.sent-2411-065: In this section, we group the related studies into two main areas.
elements.sent-2411-066: In the utilitarian framework, ~\cite{caragiannis2011voting show that the distortion of any voting rule is lower bounded by $\Omega(m^2)$, with simple rules like Plurality having a distortion of $O(m^2)$.}
elements.sent-2411-067: For the metric framework, \cite{ANSHELEVICH201827 established a general lower bound of $3$ on the distortion of any voting rule.}
elements.sent-2411-068: They also proved upper bounds on the distortion of several voting rules, including Majority, Borda, and Copeland, with the best rule being Copeland, which has a distortion of $5$.
elements.sent-2411-069: Later, ~\cite{munagala2019improved generalized this result to uncovered set rules and improved the best upper bound to $4.236$, and \cite{gkatzelis2020resolving further reduced it to $3$.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: In the study of metric distortion in multi-winner voting, various objective functions have been proposed to capture the value each voter derives from the elected committee \citep{elkind2017a, faliszewski2017multiwinner .}
elements.sent-2411-071: In the min-cost objective, each voter's cost is determined by the nearest elected candidate.
elements.sent-2411-072: They propose a voting rule with a tight distortion of $3$ and a randomized rule with a distortion of $3 - 2/m$.
elements.sent-2411-073: Another objective, introduced by \cite{caragiannis2022metric , is the $q$-cost objective, where a voter's cost for a committee is defined as the distance to their $q$-th closest member in the committee.}
elements.sent-2411-074: In metric setting, ~\cite{caragiannis2022metric show that for $q \le k/3$ distortion is unbounded, while for $k/3 < q \le k/2$, distortion is bounded by $n$.}
elements.sent-2411-075: For the case $q > k/2$, they present a non-polynomial voting rule that achieves the tight distortion of $3$.
elements.sent-2411-076: Subsequently, ~\cite{kizilkaya2022plurality propose a polynomial voting rule with the same distortion.}
elements.sent-2411-077: The study of metric multi-winner voting rules has been a recent area of focus in several other settings \citep{kalayci2024proportional, pulyassary2022algorithm, Anshelevich21KnownLocations .}

elements.sent-2411-090: For a given metric \(d\), the distance between voter \(i\) and alternative \(a\), denoted by \(d(i, a)\), represents the cost for voter \(i\) regarding alternative \(a\).

elements.sent-2411-095: A metric \(d\) is consistent with a ranking profile \(\sigma\), denoted by \(d \rhd \sigma\), if for every voter \(i\) and alternatives \(a, b\), \(d(i,a) < d(i,b)\) holds if and only if \(a \succ_i b\).

elements.sent-2411-097: The social cost of a candidate \(a\), denoted by \(\SC(a)\), is the sum of the costs for all voters, i.e., \begin{equation}\SC(a) = \sum_{i \in V} d(i,a).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-098: More generally, for a set \(S\) of alternatives, the additive social cost of \(S\) is defined as \begin{equation}\SC(S) = \sum_{a \in S}\sum_{i \in V} d(i,a).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-099: A voting rule $f$ takes a preference profile $\sigma$ as input and returns a set of $k$ alternatives as output.

elements.sent-2411-101: In this paper, we are interested in rules that minimize the additive social cost.
elements.sent-2411-102: Distortion is a measure of how efficient a voting rule is in the worst case.
elements.sent-2411-103: Given an election $\instance= (V,A,k,\sigma)$, and a metric $d \rhd \sigma$, the distortion of a committee $S$ of $k$ alternatives is defined as: \begin{equation}dist(S,\instance,d) = \frac{\SC(S)}{\min_{S'\subseteq A,|S'|=k} \SC(S')}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-104: Furthermore, the distortion of a committee $S$ of $k$ alternatives is defined as the worst-case ratio between the social cost of $S$ and the social cost of the optimal committee, where the worst-case is taken across all possible metrics, i.e., \begin{equation}\dist(S,\instance) = \sup_{d \rhd \sigma} dist(S,\instance,d).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-105: For brevity, we denote by $\opt$ the optimal committee, i.e., $\arg \min_{S' \subseteq A, |S'|=k \SC(S')$.}
elements.sent-2411-106: Furthermore, for a voting rule $f$, we define the distortion of $f$ as its worst-case output across all possible elections, i.e, \begin{equation}\dist(f) = \sup_{\instance=(V,A,k,\sigma)} \dist(f(\sigma),\instance).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-142: A natural approach to selecting a good committee is to iteratively identify and add the optimal alternative.
elements.sent-2411-143: Thus, it might be useful to characterize the optimal alternative.
elements.sent-2411-144: We show that the optimal alternative is either the nearest one to the left or the nearest one to the right of the median voter.

elements.sent-2411-298: In this section, we generalize our voting rule to $k$-winner voting rules on the line metric under an additive social cost function.
elements.sent-2411-299: We establish both upper and lower bounds on the distortion for these rules.
elements.sent-2411-300: First, we present an upper bound on the distortion of $k$-winner voting rules, which is roughly $7/3$ but may be slightly higher when $k$ is not divisible by $3$.
elements.sent-2411-301: Additionally, we derive two significant lower bounds.
elements.sent-2411-302: For $k \geq m/2$, the distortion of any $k$-winner voting rule is lower bounded by $1 + (m - k)/(3k - m)$.
elements.sent-2411-303: For $k \leq m/2$, the distortion is lower bounded by $2 + 1/k$ when $k$ is odd and $1 + \sqrt{1 + 2/k}$ when $k$ is even.
elements.sent-2411-304: These results provide a comprehensive overview of distortion behavior for $k$-winner voting rules across various values of $k$.
elements.sent-2411-305: We begin by presenting the upper bound for $k$-winner voting rules, as established by the following theorem.
elements.sent-2411-306: For every integer $k > 4$, there exists a $k$-winner voting rule $f$ with distortion upper bounded by: \begin{equation}\dist(f) \le \begin{cases} 7/3 \text{for $k \equiv 0 \pmod{3}$} 7/3 + \frac{4(\sqrt{2} - 4/3)}{k} \text{for $k \equiv 1 \pmod{3}$} 7/3 + \frac{2(\sqrt{2} - 4/3)}{k} \text{for $k \equiv 2 \pmod{3}$}. \end{cases}\end{equation} Moreover, for the case $k=4$, there exists a voting rule with distortion at most $1 + \sqrt{2}$.
elements.sent-2411-307: Before proving this theorem, we demonstrate how combining two multi-winner voting rules, each selecting different committee sizes, can achieve a $k$-winner voting rule with low distortion in Subsection \ref{Combining Voting Rules .}
elements.sent-2411-308: Subsequently, in Subsection \ref{Polar Comparison Rule for $k=3$}, we generalize the Polar Comparison Rule for the case of $k=3$, also achieving low distortion.
elements.sent-2411-309: Finally, in Subsection \ref{Proving the Upper Bound , we use the voting rules established for $k=2$ and $k=3$ to prove upper bounds on the distortion for general $k$-winner elections.}
elements.sent-2411-310: In this subsection, we present a method for combining different voting rules to create a $k$-winner selection rule.
elements.sent-2411-311: We demonstrate that the distortion of the combined rule can be expressed as a function of the distortions of the individual voting rules.
elements.sent-2411-312: This approach allows us to leverage the unique strengths of each voting rule within the selection process.
elements.sent-2411-313: Given integers $k, k_1, k_2, \alpha, \beta$ where $k = \alpha k_1 + \beta k_2$, if $f_1$ and $f_2$ are respectively $k_1$-winner and $k_2$-winner voting rules, with distortions at most $\dd_1$ and $\dd_2$, there exists a $k$-winner voting rule with distortion at most \begin{equation}\dfrac{\alpha k_1 \dd_1 + \beta k_2\dd_2}{k}.\end{equation}.

elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Polar Comparison Rule for $3$-Winner Elections} \label{alg:3-winner-voting-rule} \SetKwInOut{Input}{Input} \SetKwInOut{Output}{Output} \Output{A Committee of two alternatives $S = \{s_1, s_2\}$} $a, b_1 \gets$ the top two alternatives in \majorder $A'_1 \gets A \backslash \{ a \}$ $A'_2 \gets A \backslash \{ b_1 \}$ $B_1 \gets \{e\in A \mid \exists d\in A_1': |V_{d\succ e}| =n \}$ \tcp*{Pareto-dominated alternatives without considering alternatives that $a$ Pareto-dominates} $B_2 \gets \{e\in A \mid \exists d\in A_2': |V_{d\succ e}| =n \}$ \tcp*{Pareto-dominated alternatives without considering alternatives that $b$ Pareto-dominates} $A'_1 \gets A_1'\backslash B_1$ $A'_2 \gets A_2'\backslash B_2$ Calculate the order of alternatives as $\mathcal{S}_1$ using Algorithm \ref{alg:order-finder} with input $\instance' = (V, A_1', 2, \sigma)$. Calculate the order of alternatives as $\mathcal{S}_2$ using Algorithm \ref{alg:order-finder} with input $\instance' = (V, A_2', 2, \sigma)$. $c \gets$ nearest alternative in $\mathcal{S}_1$ to $b_1$ that is in the opposite direction of $a$ from $b_1$ $b_2 \gets$ nearest alternative in $\mathcal{S}_2$ to $a$ that is in the opposite direction of $b_1$ from $a$ \If{$c$ does not exist}{ \Return $\{ a, b_1, b_2\}$ } \If{$b_2$ does not exist }{ \Return $\{ a, b_1, c\}$ } \eIf{$|V_{c \succ b_2}| \ge \frac{2n}{5}$}{ \Return $\{a, b_1, c\}$ }{\Return $\{ a, b_1, b_2\}$ }

elements.sent-2411-521: The results presented in this section indicate that both the upper and lower bounds suggest the potential for improved distortion in voting rules as the size of the elected committee increases.
elements.sent-2411-522: Specifically, when we allow for larger values of $k$, there is a promising opportunity to develop voting rules that achieve better distortion.
elements.sent-2411-523: In this paper, we proposed a new $k$-winner voting rule and established distortion bounds for both even and odd values of $k$ in the line metric with additive social cost objective.
elements.sent-2411-524: The proposed voting rule achieves optimal distortion for $2$-winner and $3$-winner elections, bounds of $1 + \sqrt{2}$ and $7/3$, respectively, and an upper bound close to $7/3$ for general $k$-winner elections.
elements.sent-2411-525: Moreover, we proved lower bounds on the distortion of any $k$-winner voting rule when $k \le m/2$.
elements.sent-2411-526: For odd values of $k$, this lower bound is $2 + 1/k$, and for even values of $k$, it is $1 + \sqrt{1 + 2/k}$.
elements.sent-2411-528: The most significant challenge is extending our analysis to more general metric spaces.
elements.sent-2411-529: Understanding how these bounds behave in more complex settings is an important next step.
elements.sent-2411-530: Additionally, even in the line metric, tightening the bounds for general values of $k$ remains an open question.. \bibliographystyle{plainnat.}
elements.sent-2411-531: We study the problem of selecting a committee of size $k$ from a set of $m$ alternatives, based solely on the ordinal preferences of voters.
elements.sent-2411-532: Both voters and alternatives lie on the line metric, and the goal is to minimize a social cost function based on metric distances.
elements.sent-2411-533: While the distances to committee members fully determine the social cost, voting rules only have access to the ordinal preference list of each voter.
elements.sent-2411-534: The distortion of a voting rule is the worst-case ratio between the cost of the selected committee and the cost of the optimal one, over all consistent distance metrics.
elements.sent-2411-535: Extending distortion to multi-winner elections requires defining how a voter's cost is aggregated over the committee.
elements.sent-2411-536: In this work, we focus on the additive cost, where a voter's cost is the sum of their distances to all committee members.
elements.sent-2411-537: The overall social cost is either utilitarian (sum of individual costs) or egalitarian (maximum individual cost).
elements.sent-2411-538: We introduce a new voting rule, the Polar Comparison Rule, and analyze its distortion for the utilitarian additive cost.
elements.sent-2411-539: We show that it achieves a distortion of roughly $7/3$ for any committee size $k$.
elements.sent-2411-540: More specifically, for $k = 2$ and $k = 3$, we establish tight bounds of $1 + \sqrt{2 \approx 2.41$ and $7/3 \approx 2.33$, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-541: Moreover, we provide lower bounds that depend on the parity of $k$, and analyze both small and large committee sizes.
elements.sent-2411-542: Finally, we study the egalitarian additive cost and analyze the distortion bounds in multi-winner elections.
elements.sent-2411-543: Voters have cardinal costs for each candidate, but submit ordinal rankings based on these costs.
elements.sent-2411-544: The problem is how to use these rankings to form a reasonable committee.
elements.sent-2411-545: To make sense of the above scenario, we need to address two key questions.
elements.sent-2411-546: First, what defines a ``reasonable'' committee? Second, given the limitations of ordinal rankings, can we achieve—or approximately achieve—this objective without access to cardinal values? What counts as a ``reasonable'' outcome depends on what we expect from the committee and how we balance efficiency and fairness.
elements.sent-2411-547: To explore this, let us set aside the limitations of ordinal rankings for now and look at the example in Figure~\ref{fig:ub(intro) .}
elements.sent-2411-548: The goal is to select a committee of three candidates, where each voter's cost for a candidate is simply their distance from that candidate.
elements.sent-2411-549: If we aim to minimize the utilitarian social cost---that is, the total cost across all voters for all committee members---then choosing $b_1, b_2, b_3$ gives the best result.
elements.sent-2411-550: But this outcome might seem unfair.
elements.sent-2411-551: An alternative is to select $a_1, b_1, c_1$, which increases the total cost slightly but ensures that each voter's favorite candidate is included in the committee.
elements.sent-2411-552: Several well-studied criteria for multi-winner voting rules capture these trade-offs by specifying different objectives based on individual voter costs.
elements.sent-2411-553: These include minimizing the distance from each voter to their nearest committee member \citep{Chen_Li_Wang_2020, cheng2020group , the distance to their $q$th nearest member \citep{caragiannis2017subset , or the utilitarian social cost of the committee \citep{10.1145/3230654.3230658 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-554: In this paper, we focus on two classic notions from welfare economics: the utilitarian additive cost, and the egalitarian additive cost, defined as the maximum total cost any individual voter incurs for the entire committee.
elements.sent-2411-555: The second challenge is the lack of cardinal information.
elements.sent-2411-556: If cardinal values were available, finding the optimal outcome under any of the above criteria would be simple.
elements.sent-2411-557: However, assuming access to such detailed information is often unrealistic.
elements.sent-2411-558: Voters may find it difficult to precisely quantify their preferences, and requiring cardinal inputs may lead to inconsistencies, reluctance, or increased costs in data collection.
elements.sent-2411-559: For these and other reasons, ordinal rankings are preferred, despite offering less detailed information about preferences.
elements.sent-2411-560: The efficiency gap due to the lack of cardinal information is captured by term distortion \citep{procaccia2006distortion .}
elements.sent-2411-561: In single-winner elections, distortion of a voting rule $f$ is defined as the worst-case ratio (across all instances) between the social cost of the candidate selected by $f$ and that of the optimal candidate, which is based on hidden cardinal values.\footnote{We refer to Section \ref{sec:prelim for a formal definition of distortion.}}
elements.sent-2411-562: Over the past decade, distortion has been the subject of extensive studies.
elements.sent-2411-563: In general, without any assumptions on voter costs, there exist instances where the distortion can be large.
elements.sent-2411-564: As a result, much of the literature has focused on introducing structure or constraints on the cost functions to ensure bounded distortion.
elements.sent-2411-565: Among these, the metric framework has received particular attention.
elements.sent-2411-566: This setting is not only mathematically well-behaved—leading to many elegant and positive results—but also captures a wide range of real-world scenarios, such as spatial models of voting, recommendation systems, and facility location.
elements.sent-2411-567: For single-winner elections, metric distortion is very well studied.
elements.sent-2411-568: The lower bound for any deterministic rule is $3$ \citep{ANSHELEVICH201827 , and rules such as Plurality Veto \citep{kizilkaya2022plurality and Plurality Matching \citep{gkatzelis2020resolving achieve this bound.}}}
elements.sent-2411-569: Also, tight bounds have been established for many voting rules \citep{ANSHELEVICH201827 .}

elements.sent-2411-570: In this paper, we examine the distortion of selecting a committee of $k$ alternatives under the social additive cost objective.
elements.sent-2411-571: The additive cost objective is particularly relevant when all committee members influence the outcome of the committee equally.
elements.sent-2411-572: For example, in a conference, if the reviewers' combined ratings determine the final score of a paper, selecting reviewers whose expertise aligns closely with the paper minimizes the total distance between the paper's field and the reviewers' expertise.
elements.sent-2411-573: Note that committee selection with an additive cost objective is equivalent to a randomized voting rule that applies a uniform distribution over the selected candidates with a fixed support size.
elements.sent-2411-574: Thus, the outcome of this rule can also be interpreted as a randomized rule.
elements.sent-2411-575: In recent years, several studies have focused on the distortion for multi-winner elections.
elements.sent-2411-576: For the utilitarian additive cost objective in the metric framework, it has been shown by \cite{10.1145/3230654.3230658 that repeatedly applying any single-winner rule with distortion $\delta$ results in a distortion of at most $\delta$.}
elements.sent-2411-577: For instance, applying the Plurality Veto rule \citep{kizilkaya2022plurality $k$ times yields a committee of size $k$ with distortion at most $3$.}
elements.sent-2411-578: Additionally, for the $q$-cost objective,~\cite{caragiannis2022metric show that for $q \le k/3$, distortion is unbounded; for $k/3 < q \le k/2$, it is tightly bounded by $n$; and for $q \geq k/2$, it is tightly bounded by $3$.}
elements.sent-2411-579: We focus on the setting where both voters and alternatives are positioned on a line metric.
elements.sent-2411-580: Line metric is an important special case of metric space, which is widely studied \citep{black48grouDecision, moulin1980strategy, miyagawa2001locating, fotakis2016conference, fotakis2022distortion, VOUDOURIS2023266, ghodsi2019distortion .}
elements.sent-2411-581: In this setting, we improve the classic distortion bound of $3$ for the utilitarian additive cost to approximately $2.33$.
elements.sent-2411-582: The problem of selecting a committee of $k$ alternatives, based on voters' ordinal preferences, is both classical and practically significant.
elements.sent-2411-583: However, choosing more than one alternative introduces new challenges in minimizing the social cost.
elements.sent-2411-584: For minimizing the utilitarian additive cost, choosing consecutive alternatives leads to a committee with lower distortion compared to other configurations.
elements.sent-2411-585: Although the median voter seems to be a good representative--and indeed, if selecting a single alternative, the nearest alternative to either the left or right of the median voter would be optimal--returning the entire committee based solely on the preference list of the median voter cannot achieve a distortion better than $3$.
elements.sent-2411-586: This is because the median voter might prefer all alternatives on one side of their location to all alternatives on the other side.
elements.sent-2411-587: Therefore, it is important to choose alternatives from both sides of the median voter.
elements.sent-2411-588: To leverage this insight, we design a new voting rule, the Polar Comparison Rule, which prioritizes committees that include alternatives from both sides of the median voter, thereby increasing the likelihood of selecting better alternatives.
elements.sent-2411-589: We first introduce this voting rule for selecting a committee of size two.
elements.sent-2411-590: Specifically, the rule selects the top-ranked alternative of the median voter and then compares the two closest alternatives to the median voter on opposite sides of each other that have not yet been picked.
elements.sent-2411-591: Based on the ratio of voters who prefer one alternative to the other, the rule selects the alternative that is more preferred, while introducing a bias toward the alternative on the opposite side of the previously chosen alternative.
elements.sent-2411-592: We then extend this voting rule to select a committee of size three and generalize it to any committee size by iteratively applying the rules for $k=3$ and $k=2$.
elements.sent-2411-593: We further extend these results by showing how different voting rules can be effectively combined to improve the distortion bounds for various committee sizes.
elements.sent-2411-594: Specifically, we generalize our rule to maintain a distortion of $7/3$ for committee sizes divisible by three.
elements.sent-2411-595: For committee sizes where $k \equiv 1 \pmod{3}$, the distortion becomes $7/3 + 4(\sqrt{2 - 4/3)/k$, and for committee sizes where $k \equiv 2 \pmod{3}$, the distortion becomes $7/3 + 2(\sqrt{2 - 4/3)/k$.}}
elements.sent-2411-596: Additionally, we complement these results with lower bounds.
elements.sent-2411-597: For each lower bound, we provide two instances where voters share the same preference profile and demonstrate a constraint that any voting rule must satisfy.
elements.sent-2411-598: We establish a lower bound of $2 + 1/k$ for odd values of $k$ and $1 + \sqrt{1 + 2/k}$ for even values of $k$, when $k < m/2$.
elements.sent-2411-599: Furthermore, for larger committee sizes where $k \ge m/2$, we derive a lower bound of $1 + (m - k)/(3k - m)$.

elements.sent-2411-600: To establish our results, we rely on several techniques that can provide valuable insight beyond the specific context of this problem.
elements.sent-2411-601: One useful technique involves determining the order of alternatives on the line metric after removing all alternatives that are Pareto-dominated.
elements.sent-2411-602: Another key observation arises from analyzing how voter movements affect distortion.
elements.sent-2411-603: Specifically, we note that in any instance moving a single voter to either the right or left results in a new instance with worse distortion.
elements.sent-2411-604: While this observation does not directly aid our proofs, it prompts us to explore movements that yield instances with guaranteed properties relative to the distortion of the initial instance.
elements.sent-2411-605: We show that there is a specific point on the line such that moving all voters toward this point results in a new instance where the distortion does not significantly decrease.
elements.sent-2411-606: To apply this technique effectively, we define certain obstacles that restrict how far voters can move toward this point.
elements.sent-2411-607: Each voter moves until encountering an obstacle, at which point they stop.
elements.sent-2411-608: This approach allows us to precisely determine the structure of the resulting instance.
elements.sent-2411-609: Importantly, the placement of these obstacles depends on the voting rule being analyzed.
elements.sent-2411-610: For our Polar Comparison Rule, we position these obstacles at the midpoint between the two alternatives being compared.
elements.sent-2411-611: This ensures that the resulting instance aligns with the properties of the rule and facilitates accurate analysis of distortion.
elements.sent-2411-612: Moreover, we study the egalitarian additive cost and prove that any committee selecting alternatives between the two extreme voters achieves a distortion of $2$.
elements.sent-2411-613: We first show that the maximum cost is incurred by either the leftmost or rightmost voter, and then bound both the cost of our selected committee and the optimal one in terms of the distance between these two extreme voters.

elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{Finding the Order of Alternatives} \label{alg:order-finder} Input: Instance $\instance = (V, A, k, \sigma)$ Output: A sequence of non-Pareto-dominated alternatives, $\mathcal{S}$, representing their ordering on the real line Select $a, b \in A $ \textcolor{blue}{\tcp*{Arbitrary alternatives for partitioning}} $R \gets \{c \mid c \in A, V_{a\succ b} \subseteq V_{a\succ c} \}$\; $L \gets A \backslash R$\; $i \gets $ a voter whose top vote is in $L$\; $j \gets $ a voter whose top vote is in $R$\; $\mathcal{S}_L \gets \sigma_i(L)$ \textcolor{blue}{\tcp*{Order $L$ by voter $i$'s preferences}} $\mathcal{S}_R \gets \sigma_j(R)$ \textcolor{blue}{\tcp*{Order $R$ by voter $j$'s preferences}} $\mathcal{S} \gets \text{reverse}(\mathcal{S}_L) \cdot \mathcal{S}_R$ \textcolor{blue}{\tcp*{Reverse $L$'s order and concatenate with $R$}} \Return $\mathcal{S}$\;

elements.algorithm-2411-005: \caption{Finding the \focalpoint} \label{alg:move-voters-finding-point} \SetKwInOut{Input}{Input} \SetKwInOut{Output}{Output} \Output{A \focalpoint $x^*$ on the line with respect to $S_1$ and $S_2$.} $C = S_1 \cap S_2 = \{ c_1, c_2, \dots , c_t\}$ \textcolor{blue}{\tcp*{Assuming $x_{c_i} \le x_{c_{i + 1}}$}} $S_1 \setminus C = \{ b_1, b_2, \dots, b_{k - t}\}$ \textcolor{blue}{\tcp*{Assuming $x_{b_i} \ge x_{b_{i + 1}}$ and $x_{b_i} \ge x_{c_j}$}} $S_2 \setminus C = \{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_{k - t}\}$ \textcolor{blue}{\tcp*{Assuming $x_{a_i} \le x_{a_{i + 1}}$ and $x_{a_i} \le x_{c_j}$}} $r \gets |C| - \left \lfloor k/2 \right\rfloor$\; $j^* \gets \left\lceil k(\dd - 1)/(2\dd) \right\rceil $\; \eIf{$r < 0$}{ \Return $x_{b_{j^*}}$\; }{ \eIf{$k$ is even}{ $i^* \gets \left \lfloor (k - 2r)/(2(\dd - 1))\right \rfloor$\; $\hat{\dd} \gets k/(2r)$ \; }{ $i^* \gets \left \lfloor (k - 2r - \dd)/(2(\dd - 1)) \right \rfloor$\; $\hat{\dd} \gets k/(2r + 1)$ \; } \eIf{$\dd \le \hat{\dd}$}{ \Return $ x_{b_{j^*}}$\; }{ \Return $x_{c_{\left \lceil k/2 \right \rceil + i^*}}$\; } }

elements.algorithm-2411-007: \caption{Polar Comparison Rule for $3$-Winner Elections} \label{alg:3-winner-voting-rule} \SetKwInOut{Input}{Input} \SetKwInOut{Output}{Output} \Output{A Committee of two alternatives $S = \{s_1, s_2\}$} $a, b_1 \gets$ the top two alternatives in the \majorder\; $A'_1 \gets A \backslash \{ a \}$\; $A'_2 \gets A \backslash \{ b_1 \}$\; $B_1 \gets \{e\in A \mid \exists e'\in A_1': |V_{e'\succ e}| =n \}$ \textcolor{blue}{\tcp*{Alternatives Pareto-dominated by $A_1'$}} $B_2 \gets \{e\in A \mid \exists e'\in A_2': |V_{e'\succ e}| =n \}$ \textcolor{blue}{\tcp*{Exclude alternatives Pareto-dominated by $b$}} $A'_1 \gets A_1'\backslash B_1$\; $A'_2 \gets A_2'\backslash B_2$\; Find $c \in A_1'$ minimizing $d(c, b_1)$ s.t. $b_1$ lies between $c$ and $a$ \textcolor{blue}{\tcp*{Using Lemma \ref{lem:alternatives-order}}} Find $b_2 \in A_2'$ minimizing $d(b_2, a)$ s.t. $b_1$ lies between $a$ and $b_2$ \textcolor{blue}{\tcp*{Using Lemma \ref{lem:alternatives-order}}} \If{$c$ does not exist}{ \Return $\{ a, b_1, b_2\}$\; } \If{$b_2$ does not exist }{ \Return $\{ a, b_1, c\}$ \; } \eIf{$|V_{c \succ b_2}| \ge \frac{2n}{5}$}{ \Return $\{a, b_1, c\}$\; }{\Return $\{ a, b_1, b_2\}$\; }
